docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Experimental site

This is an experimental documentation site with AI-generated draft content. It is not the official AICR documentation. Content here has not been fully reviewed and may contain errors. The official site is docs.aicr.ai.

Welcome to the documentation for the Massachusetts AI Compute Resource (AICR) — a multi-institutional GPU cluster for AI research, serving Boston University, Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, UMass (five campuses), and Yale. Part of the Massachusetts AI Hub, housed at the MGHPCC in Holyoke, MA.

Get Started

New to AICR? Start here.

  • Quick Start
  • Tutorial
  • System Overview
  • Requesting Access
Run Jobs

Submit and manage compute jobs.

  • Slurm Basics
  • GPU Jobs
  • Job Arrays
  • Monitoring
AI Tools

PyTorch, JAX, Hugging Face, vLLM, and more.

  • AI Tools Overview
  • PyTorch
  • Distributed Training
  • Inference & Serving
GPUs

248 B200 + 152 RTX PRO 6000 GPUs.

  • GPU Overview
  • Getting Started with GPUs
  • Multi-GPU Computing
  • GPU Profiling
Files & Data

Storage, quotas, snapshots, transfers.

  • Filesystem Overview
  • Transferring Data
  • Managing Quotas
  • Snapshots
Help

FAQ, troubleshooting, support contacts.

  • Getting Help
  • FAQ
  • Login Troubleshooting
  • About AICR

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
Definitions of HPC and AI terms used throughout this documentation. Terms are listed alphabetically.

Account (Slurm) An organizational unit in Slurm tied to your institution. You specify it with --account when submitting jobs. AICR accounts include bu, hu, mit, neu, umass, uml, umb, umassd, umassmed, yale, ma, and aihub. Apptainer A container runtime for HPC (formerly Singularity). Lets you package an entire software environment into a single .sif file and run it on the cluster without root privileges. See Containers. Batch Job A job submitted with sbatch that runs unattended. You write a job script, submit it, and collect the results later. Contrast with an interactive job. Checkpoint A saved snapshot of a running job's state (model weights, optimizer state, epoch number). Allows long-running jobs to resume after a timeout or failure. See Checkpointing. Compute Node A machine in the cluster where your jobs actually run. You do not log into compute nodes directly — the scheduler assigns them to your jobs. Conda A package and environment manager commonly used for Python and R. Lets you create isolated environments with specific package versions. See Python and Conda. Container A packaged software environment that includes an OS, libraries, and applications in a single file. On AICR, containers are run with Apptainer. See Containers. CPU Central Processing Unit. On AICR, the cpu partition provides nodes with 128 cores and 1 TB memory each, intended for data analysis and workflow orchestration. CUDA NVIDIA's programming platform for GPUs. Required for running GPU-accelerated code. The CUDA toolkit is available as a module on AICR. DDP (Distributed Data Parallel) A PyTorch strategy for training a model across multiple GPUs. Each GPU holds a copy of the model and processes a different data shard. Gradients are synchronized after each step. See Distributed Training. Fairshare Slurm's mechanism for allocating resources fairly across a three-level hierarchy: Institution → Project → User. If your institution and project have used less than their share recently, your jobs get higher priority. You do not need to manage this yourself. FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) A PyTorch strategy for training models that are too large for a single GPU's memory. The model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states are sharded across GPUs. See Distributed Training. Globus A data transfer service for moving large files between systems. Transfers continue in the background — you do not need to stay logged in. See Transferring Data. GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) A processor designed for parallel computation. AICR has NVIDIA B200 and RTX PRO 6000 GPUs, 8 per node. Used for training and running AI models. See GPU Overview. GRES (Generic Resources) Slurm's mechanism for requesting specialized resources like GPUs. You can request GPUs with --gpus=2 or --gres=gpu:2 (both are equivalent). Home Directory Your personal storage at /home/USERNAME. 100 GiB quota with 7-day snapshots. For code, configs, and small files. See Filesystem Overview. InfiniBand A high-speed network connecting AICR nodes. NDR400 InfiniBand provides 400 Gb/s bandwidth for GPU-to-GPU communication and storage access. Interactive Job A job started with salloc that gives you a shell on a compute node for real-time development and testing. Limited to 4 concurrent jobs on devel partitions. Job A unit of work submitted to the scheduler. A job specifies what resources you need (CPUs, GPUs, memory, time) and what to run. See Slurm Basics. Job Array A way to submit many similar jobs from a single script. Each task gets a unique ID via $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID. Useful for parameter sweeps. See Job Arrays. Job Script A bash script containing #SBATCH directives (resource requests) and the commands to run. Submitted with sbatch. Login Node The machine you connect to when you SSH into the cluster. For editing files, submitting jobs, and light tasks only — not for running computation. Limited to 4 CPU cores and 8 GB memory per user. Lmod The module system used on AICR. Manages software by modifying your shell environment. See Modules. MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) An NVIDIA technology that partitions a physical GPU into smaller, isolated instances. May be available on some AICR nodes for lighter workloads. Module A software package managed by Lmod. Loading a module makes the software available in your session. Commands: module avail, module load, module purge. MPI (Message Passing Interface) A standard for communication between processes running on different nodes. Used for multi-node parallel jobs. AICR uses PMIx for MPI process management. NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) A library for fast GPU-to-GPU communication. Used by PyTorch DDP and FSDP for gradient synchronization during distributed training. Node A single machine in the cluster. AICR has GPU nodes (a-series and b-series), CPU nodes (w-series), and service nodes. OnDemand A web portal for accessing the cluster through your browser. Provides a file manager, terminal, and interactive applications without SSH. See OnDemand. Partition A group of nodes in Slurm with shared resource limits. AICR has five partitions: cpu, rtx-batch, rtx-devel, b200-batch, b200-devel. You choose a partition with --partition. PMIx Process Management Interface for Exascale. The process launcher used by AICR's Slurm for MPI jobs. Specify with srun --mpi=pmix. QoS (Quality of Service) A Slurm mechanism that sets job limits. The interactive QoS on devel partitions limits users to 4 concurrent jobs. RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) A networking technology that transfers data directly between memories without involving the CPU. AICR uses NFS-RDMA over InfiniBand for fast storage access. Scratch Temporary high-capacity storage at /scratch/USERNAME. ~10 TiB quota with ~15M file limit, no snapshots, subject to regular purging (~30 days). For active job data only. See Filesystem Overview. Scheduler Software that manages compute resources and runs your jobs on available nodes. AICR uses Slurm 25.11.1. Slurm The workload manager and job scheduler used on AICR. Commands include sbatch, salloc, srun, squeue, sacct, scancel. See Slurm Basics. Snapshot A read-only point-in-time copy of your files. Available on Home and Work storage (not Scratch). Used to recover accidentally deleted files. See Restoring from Snapshots. SSH (Secure Shell) A protocol for connecting to remote computers over a network. The primary way to access AICR from a terminal. AICR uses SSH certificate authentication with short-lived CA-signed certificates. See SSH. vLLM An open-source inference engine for large language models. Supports multi-GPU serving with tensor parallelism. See Inference and Serving. Wall Time The maximum real-world time a job is allowed to run, specified with --time. If a job exceeds its wall time, it is terminated. Work Directory Shared storage for your institution at /work/INSTITUTION/PROJECTNAME. Per-project quota with 7-day snapshots. For project data and collaboration. See Filesystem Overview.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: If you have HPC experience and just need the AICR specifics, this page has everything to get you running. New to HPC? Start with the Getting Started Tutorial instead.

docs.title: Connect
docs.text:
SSHOnDemand 
$ ssh USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

SSH certificate authentication. Use the certificate files provided when your account was created. No password. See SSH setup.

Open the AICR OnDemand portal in your browser and log in with your institutional credentials.

docs.title: Storage at a Glance
docs.text:
Storage Path Quota Snapshots Purge Home /home/USERNAME 100 GiB 7-day None Scratch /scratch/USERNAME ~10 TiB None Regular Work /work/INSTITUTION/PROJECT Varies 7-day None

Warning

No off-site backups. Back up irreplaceable files externally.

More details: Filesystem Overview

docs.title: Partitions
docs.text:
Partition GPUs/Node GPU Model Max Time Default Time Use Case cpu — — 24h 15 min Analysis, data processing rtx-batch 8 RTX PRO 6000 24h 1h RTX GPU batch jobs rtx-devel 8 RTX PRO 6000 4h 15 min RTX GPU testing b200-batch 8 NVIDIA B200 24h 1h B200 GPU batch jobs b200-devel 8 NVIDIA B200 4h 15 min B200 GPU testing

Default memory: 1 GB per CPU. Always specify --account=ACCOUNT_NAME (your institution's Slurm account).

docs.title: Submit a Job
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-devel
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=16G
#SBATCH --time=00:30:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

python my_script.py

$ sbatch my_job.sh

docs.title: Interactive Session
docs.text: $ salloc --partition=rtx-devel --gpus=1 --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=16G --time=01:00:00 --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
$ module avail          # list available software
$ module load cuda      # load a module
$ module list           # see what's loaded
$ module purge          # reset to clean state

docs.title: Check Your Jobs
docs.text:
$ squeue -u $USER       # your running/pending jobs
$ sacct -j JOBID        # completed job details
$ scancel JOBID         # cancel a job

docs.title: How to Request Access
docs.text:
AICR is currently available to initial beta-test users. Each institution may have its own process for getting an account. When it is available we will include links to that information here.

AICR accounts are managed through your institution's research computing team. Contact them to get started:

Institution Research Computing Group Slurm Account Boston University BU Research Computing Services bu Harvard University Harvard FAS Research Computing hu MIT MIT ORCD mit Northeastern University Northeastern RC neu UMass Amherst UMass Unity RC umass UMass Boston UMass Unity RC umb UMass Dartmouth UMass Unity RC umassd UMass Lowell UMass Unity RC uml UMass Medical UMass Unity RC umassmed Yale University Yale YCRC yale

Note

Your AICR username follows the format institutionalusername_institutioncode (e.g., jsmith_mit for a user jsmith at MIT). It will be provided when your account is created.

docs.title: AICR System Overview
docs.text: AICR is a GPU-focused AI computing cluster housed at the MGHPCC in Holyoke, MA. It serves researchers at Boston University, Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, UMass (five campuses), and Yale as part of the Massachusetts AI Hub.

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text: Component Count Details More Info NVIDIA B200 GPU Nodes 31 8 B200 GPUs per node (248 total), 180 GB HBM3e per GPU, NVLink 5, liquid-cooled Details NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 GPU Nodes 19 8 RTX PRO 6000 GPUs per node (152 total), 96 GB GDDR7 per GPU, PCIe Gen5 Details Service Nodes 32 Dell R6715, 128-core AMD EPYC 9745, 1.125 TB RAM, login/Slurm/OnDemand/Globus Details Storage ~14.3 PiB effective VAST Data all-flash, 360 GB/s throughput, encryption at rest, GPU Direct Storage Details Networking 4 fabrics Rail-optimized IB (B200 GPU), cluster IB (storage/MPI), Ethernet (management), OOB (control) Details

docs.title: Partitions
docs.text:
Partition Hardware GPUs/Node Max Time Use Case cpu w-series (128 cores, 1 TB) — 24h Data analysis, workflow orchestration rtx-batch a-series 8 RTX PRO 6000 24h RTX GPU batch workloads rtx-devel a-series 8 RTX PRO 6000 4h Interactive GPU development b200-batch b-series 8 B200 24h B200 GPU batch workloads b200-devel b-series 8 B200 4h Interactive GPU development

Default memory per CPU: 1 GB. Development partitions have a 4-concurrent-job limit per user.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Storage Path Quota Snapshots Purge Home /home/USERNAME 100 GiB 7-day None Scratch /scratch/USERNAME ~10 TiB None Regular Work /work/INSTITUTION/PROJECT Varies 7-day None

Warning

AICR storage relies on snapshots only — there are no off-site backups. Back up irreplaceable data externally.

docs.title: Interconnect
docs.text: AICR nodes are connected by InfiniBand NDR400 (400 Gb/s) for high-bandwidth, low-latency communication between GPUs and storage. This enables fast distributed training across multiple GPU nodes and high-throughput access to the VAST storage system via NFS-RDMA.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Quick Start — get running on AICR in 5 minutes
  • Slurm Basics — how to submit jobs
  • Filesystem Overview — storage details
  • GPU Overview — GPU hardware and partitions

docs.title: Getting Started Tutorial
docs.text: This page walks through setting up and getting started with your AICR account step by step. If you already have HPC experience and just need the AICR specifics, see the Quick Start instead.

docs.title: Getting an Account
docs.text: AICR is currently available to initial beta-test users. Each institution may have its own process for getting an account. When it is available we will include links to that information here. See Requesting Access for more details.

docs.title: Logging In
docs.text: The first thing you should do when you get a new account is verify that you can log in. You can log in using either the AICR OnDemand portal or through SSH in the terminal. See the SSH and OnDemand pages for more details.

docs.title: OnDemand Portal
docs.text: You can log into OnDemand Web Portal using your institutional credentials. Once the OnDemand Portal is available we will put the URL here.

docs.title: Terminal with SSH
docs.text:
Log into AICR with the following command in a terminal window. Replace USERNAME below with your AICR username.

$ ssh USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

Your AICR username will differ from your institutional username — it follows the format institutionalusername_institutioncode (e.g., jsmith_mit for a user jsmith at MIT). It was provided when your account was created. AICR uses SSH certificate authentication — you received a certificate file when your account was set up. See SSH for details on configuring your SSH client.

docs.title: Shared HPC Clusters
docs.text:
AICR is a shared HPC cluster. You are sharing this resource with a number of other researchers, staff, and students, so it is important that you read this page and use the system as intended.

Being a cluster, there are several machines connected together with a network. We refer to these as nodes. Most nodes in the cluster are referred to as compute nodes, this is where the computation is done on the system (where you will run your code). When you ssh into the system you are on a special purpose node called the login node. The login node, as its name suggests, is where you log in and is for editing code and files, installing packages and software, downloading data, and starting jobs to run your code on one of the compute nodes.

Each job is started using a piece of software called the scheduler, which you can think of as a resource manager. You let it know what resources you need and what you want to run, and the scheduler will find those resources and start your job on them. When your job completes those resources are relinquished. The scheduler is what ensures that no two jobs are using the same resources, so it is very important not to run anything unless it is submitted properly through the scheduler.

docs.title: Software and Packages
docs.text:
The first thing you may want to do is make sure the system has the software and packages you need. We have installed a lot of software and packages on the system already, even though it may not be immediately obvious that it is there. AICR uses modules to manage software, which should be similar to your home institution's system. AICR has a common set of core modules shared across institutions, as well as institution-specific modules whose naming and layout may be more familiar to you. Institution-specific modules are available to all AICR users, but may be hidden by default.

Run module avail to see the default set of modules available to you. You can load a module with the module load command. Please see the Software page for more information.

If you are ever unsure if we have a particular software, and you cannot find it, you can try installing it in your home directory. If you need help, please send us an email and ask before you spend a lot of time trying to install it. If we have it, we can point you to it, provide advice on how to use it, and if we don't have it we can often give pointers on how to install it. Further, if a lot of people request the same software, we may consider adding it as a module.

docs.title: Linux Command Line
docs.text:
AICR runs Linux, so much of what you do on the cluster involves the Linux command line. That doesn't mean you have to be a Linux expert to use the system! However the more you can get comfortable with the Linux command line and a handful of basic commands, the easier using the system will be. If you are already familiar with Linux, feel free to skip this section, or skim as a refresher.

Most Linux commands deal with directories and files. A directory, synonymous to a folder, contains files and other directories. The list of directories that lead to a particular directory or file is called its path. In Linux, directories on a path are separated by forward slashes /. It is also important to note that everything in Linux is case sensitive, so a file myScript.sh is not the same as the file myscript.sh. When you first log in, you are in your home directory. Your home directory is where you can put all the code and data you need to run your job. Your home directory is not accessible to other users, so if you need a space to share files with other users, use your institution's Work directory at /work/INSTITUTION (see Filesystem Overview).

The path to your home directory on AICR is /home/USERNAME, where USERNAME is your username. The character ~ is also shorthand for your home directory in any Linux commands.

Anytime after you start typing a Linux command you can press the "Tab" button on your keyboard. This is called tab-complete, and will try to autocomplete what you are typing. This is particularly helpful when typing out long directory paths and file names. Pressing "Tab" once will complete if there is a single completion, pressing it twice will list all potential completions.

docs.title: Common Linux Commands
docs.text:
Creating, navigating and viewing directories:

  • pwd — tells you the full path of the directory you are currently in
  • mkdir dirname — creates a directory with the name "dirname"
  • cd dirname — change directory to directory "dirname"
    • cd ../ — takes you up one level
  • ls — lists the files in the directory
    • ls -a — lists all files including hidden files
    • ls -l — lists files in "long format" including ownership and date of last update
    • ls -t — lists files by date stamp, most recently updated file first
    • ls -tr — lists files by date stamp in reverse order, most recently updated file is listed last (this is useful if you have a lot of files, you want to know which file you changed last and the list of files results in a scrolling window)
    • ls dirname — lists the files in the directory "dirname"

Viewing files:

  • more filename — shows the first part of a file, hitting the space bar allows you to scroll through the rest of the file, q will cause you to exit out of the file
  • less filename — allows you to scroll through the file, forward and backward, using the arrow keys
  • tail filename — shows the last 10 lines of a file (useful when you are monitoring a log file or output file to see that the values are correct)
    • tail -n NUMBER filename — shows you the last NUMBER lines of a file
    • tail -f filename — shows you new lines as they are written to the end of the file. Press Ctrl+C to exit. This is helpful to monitor the log file of a batch job.

Copying, moving, renaming, and deleting files:

  • mv filename dirname — moves filename to directory dirname
    • mv filename1 filename2 — moves filename1 to filename2, in essence renames the file. The date and time are not changed by the mv command.
  • cp filename dirname — copies to directory dirname
    • cp filename1 filename2 — copies filename1 to filename2. The date stamp on filename2 will be the date/time that the file was copied.
    • cp -r dirname1 dirname2 — copies directory dirname1 and its contents to dirname2.
  • rm filename — removes (deletes) the file

docs.title: Transferring Files
docs.text:
One of the first tasks is to get your code, data, and any other files you need into your home directory on the system. Review the Transferring Data page for a full description of how to move data on and off AICR.

If your code is in GitHub you can use git commands on the system to clone your repository to your home directory. The primary method we recommend for copying your files to and from AICR is through Globus. You can also transfer your files from your computer using the OnDemand File Browser or by using the commands scp or rsync.

You can use scp or rsync from the command line on your local computer. Both commands work similarly to the cp command, following the pattern command source destination, the only difference being that you will need to include the hostname of the system you are transferring to or from. For this reason you must run this command from the terminal on your computer before you've logged in.

To transfer a file from your computer to AICR:

$ scp LOCAL_FILE USERNAME@transfer.aicr.ai:PATH_TO_AICR_DIR

To transfer a file from AICR to your computer:

$ scp USERNAME@transfer.aicr.ai:PATH_TO_AICR_DIR/FILENAME PATH_TO_LOCAL_DIR

Similar to cp, use the -r flag to copy over an entire directory and its contents.

$ scp -r LOCAL_DIR USERNAME@transfer.aicr.ai:PATH_TO_AICR_DIR

The rsync command can be used similarly and has some additional flags you can use. It also can be used to transfer only new or modified files to the destination, which makes it easy to keep a directory in "sync".

docs.title: Running your First Job
docs.text:
At this point you may want to do a test-run of your code. You always want to start small in your test runs, so you should choose a small example that tests the functionality of what you would ultimately like to run on the system. If your test code is serial and runs okay on a moderate personal laptop or desktop you can request an interactive session to run your code in by executing the command:

# Requesting four cores, one RTX PRO 6000 GPU, and 16 GB memory for 1 hour
$ salloc --partition=rtx-devel --cpus-per-task=4 --gpus=1 --mem=16G --time=01:00:00 --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

Replace ACCOUNT_NAME with your institution's Slurm account (e.g., mit, bu, yale). See the Glossary for the full list.

After you run this command you will be on a compute node and you can do a test-run of your code. This command will allocate four CPU cores, one RTX PRO 6000 GPU, and 16 GB of memory to your job. If your test code needs additional cores or uses a lot of memory, you should request additional resources as needed.

We provide the basics of running jobs on the Running Jobs page. For more information you can consult the documentation at your home institution.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text: AICR is being supported by the staff at your home institution's research computing group. If you need help or have any questions you can reach out to them. See Getting Help for more information.

docs.title: NVIDIA B200 GPU Nodes
docs.text: The B200 nodes are AICR's primary AI training resource — 31 Dell PowerEdge XE9685L liquid-cooled servers, each containing 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs connected by NVLink 5. Together they provide 248 B200 GPUs with 43.6 TB of GPU memory and over 2 ExaFLOPS of FP8 training throughput.

docs.title: Per-Node Specifications
docs.text: Component Specification Server Dell PowerEdge XE9685L (4U, liquid-cooled) CPUs 2x AMD EPYC 9575F (Turin, Zen 5) — 128 cores / 256 threads, 3.30 GHz GPUs 8x NVIDIA B200 (SXM, Blackwell) — CUDA Compute Capability 10.0 GPU Memory 180 GB HBM3e per GPU — 1,440 GB (1.4 TB) per node GPU Memory Bandwidth 8 TB/s per GPU — 64 TB/s per node System RAM 24x 96 GB DDR5-6400 = 2,304 GB (2.25 TB) Local NVMe 8x 3.84 TB = 30.72 TB CUDA Cores 18,432 per GPU Tensor Cores 576 per GPU (5th generation, FP4/FP6/FP8 micro-tensor scaling) NVLink 5th gen, 1,800 GB/s bidirectional per GPU NVSwitch 14.4 TB/s aggregate (all-to-all topology within node) Power 12,200 W max per node Cooling Direct liquid cooling (Vertiv XDU600 CDU)

docs.title: Network Interfaces
docs.text:
Interface Speed Purpose 8x ConnectX-7 NDR 400 Gb/s each GPU data fabric (rail-optimized, 1 per GPU) 1x ConnectX-7 NDR 400 Gb/s Cluster IB fabric (VAST storage access) 2x 25 GbE 25 Gb/s each In-band management / provisioning 1x 1 GbE (iDRAC) 1 Gb/s Out-of-band management

Each node has 9 InfiniBand connections: 8 for the GPU data fabric (one per GPU, rail-optimized) and 1 for storage. This separation keeps GPU training traffic off the storage network.

docs.title: Intra-Node: NVLink 5 and NVSwitch
docs.text:
Inside each node, the 8 GPUs communicate through NVLink 5 via an NVSwitch chip on the HGX baseboard. Every GPU can talk to every other at 1,800 GB/s — full bisection bandwidth with no "near" or "far" GPU penalty.

For perspective, NVLink 5 at 1,800 GB/s is roughly 28x faster than a PCIe Gen5 x16 link. Communication between GPUs within a node is effectively free compared to computation time. This makes tensor parallelism (splitting layers across GPUs) practical within a single node.

docs.title: Inter-Node: Rail-Optimized InfiniBand
docs.text:
The 31 B200 nodes are connected by a dedicated rail-optimized NDR InfiniBand fabric following NVIDIA's SuperPOD reference design.

In a rail-optimized topology, each node's 8 GPU NICs connect to 8 different leaf switches — all GPU[0]s across every node connect to the same leaf ("Rail 0"), all GPU[1]s to another leaf ("Rail 1"), and so on. During data-parallel training, each GPU synchronizes gradients with the same-numbered GPU on other nodes, so the allreduce traffic stays within a single leaf switch hop.

Property Value Topology Rail-optimized leaf-spine Leaf switches 10x NVIDIA QM9790 (NDR) Spine switches 2x NVIDIA QM9700 (NDR) Node-to-leaf speed NDR 400 Gb/s per link Network bandwidth per node 3,200 Gb/s (8 x 400 Gb/s = 400 GB/s)

docs.title: Per-GPU Performance
docs.text:
Precision Peak Throughput Typical Use FP64 Tensor 37 TFLOPS HPC with tensor acceleration FP32 75 TFLOPS General compute TF32 Tensor 2,250 TFLOPS Default training precision BF16 / FP16 Tensor 4,500 TFLOPS Mixed-precision training FP8 Tensor 9,000 TFLOPS Transformer training, quantized inference FP4 Tensor 18,000 TFLOPS Low-precision inference

Tensor Core figures include structured sparsity (2:4). Non-sparse values are half the listed numbers.

docs.title: Aggregate (31 Nodes, 248 GPUs)
docs.text:
Resource Total GPU Memory (HBM3e) 43.6 TB GPU Memory Bandwidth ~2 PB/s System RAM (DDR5) ~70 TB FP8 Tensor Performance 2.23 EFLOP/s BF16 Tensor Performance 1.12 EFLOP/s CPU Cores 3,968 Local NVMe 952 TB Power (servers only) ~378 kW

The 43.6 TB of aggregate GPU memory can hold frontier-scale models (hundreds of billions of parameters) distributed across the cluster, or fit models with tens of billions of parameters entirely within a single node's 1.4 TB.

docs.title: Slurm Partitions
docs.text: Partition Nodes Max Time Default Time Use Case b200-batch b0001–b0028 24h 1h Production training b200-devel b0029–b0031 4h 15 min Development and testing

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• RTX PRO 6000 Nodes — the Tier-2 GPU nodes
  • Networking — fabric topology details
  • GPU Overview — choosing between B200 and RTX
  • Distributed Training — using multi-node B200 training

docs.title: Networking
docs.text: AICR uses four distinct network fabrics, each purpose-built for a specific traffic class. Two are InfiniBand (high-performance compute and storage) and two are Ethernet (management and hardware control). This separation ensures that GPU training traffic, storage I/O, management operations, and hardware control never compete for the same links.

docs.title: Fabric Overview
docs.text: Fabric Technology Switches Purpose Connected Nodes B200 Rail-Optimized IB NDR InfiniBand 12 (2 spine + 10 leaf) GPU-to-GPU training across B200 nodes B200 only Cluster IB NDR InfiniBand 6 (2 spine + 4 leaf) Storage (VAST), MPI, all nodes All Core Ethernet 25/100/400 GbE 2x SN4700 (MLAG pair) Management, provisioning, user access All Out-of-Band 1 GbE 9x SN2201 (1 per rack) BMC/iDRAC hardware management All

docs.title: Per-Node Connectivity
docs.text: Node Type Count Rail-Optimized IB Cluster IB Core Ethernet OOB B200 31 8x NDR 400G 1x NDR 400G 2x 25 GbE 1x 1 GbE RTX PRO 6000 19 — 2x NDR 400G 2x 25 GbE 1x 1 GbE Service 32 — 1x NDR 400G 2x 100 GbE 1x 1 GbE

docs.title: 1. B200 Rail-Optimized InfiniBand
docs.text: The highest-performance fabric in the cluster. It carries GPU-to-GPU traffic for distributed training across the 31 B200 nodes exclusively — no other node type connects to it.

docs.title: Rail Optimization
docs.text:
Each B200 node has 8 InfiniBand NICs, one per GPU. In the rail-optimized layout, all GPU[0]s across every node connect to the same leaf switch ("Rail 0"), all GPU[1]s to another leaf ("Rail 1"), and so on through Rail 7. This aligns with data-parallel training, where each GPU synchronizes gradients with the corresponding GPU on other nodes — that allreduce traffic stays within a single leaf switch hop.

Property Value Topology Rail-optimized leaf-spine (SuperPOD reference) Leaf switches 10x NVIDIA QM9790 (NDR, 800G-capable) Spine switches 2x NVIDIA QM9700 (NDR) Node-to-leaf speed NDR 400 Gb/s per link Spine-leaf speed NDR 800 Gb/s (bonded 2x400G) Total node ports 248 (31 nodes x 8 GPUs) Bandwidth per node 3,200 Gb/s (400 GB/s aggregate) Managed by 2x NVIDIA R660 UFM appliances

docs.title: Expansion
docs.text: The current 31 nodes nearly fill one NVIDIA Scalable Unit (32-node capacity). The spine switches already support a second SU, so the cluster can grow to 63 B200 nodes (504 GPUs) by adding leaf switches and cabling — no recabling of existing nodes required.

docs.title: 2. Cluster InfiniBand
docs.text:
The cluster-wide general-purpose InfiniBand network connecting every node type — B200, RTX, service, and VAST storage. It carries storage I/O and MPI communication for RTX multi-node jobs.

Property Value Topology Leaf-spine Leaf switches 4x NVIDIA QM9790 Spine switches 2x NVIDIA QM9700 Total endpoint ports 133 Aggregate endpoint bandwidth 5,950 GB/s Managed by 2x NVIDIA R660 UFM appliances

docs.title: Connected Endpoints
docs.text:
Endpoint Ports Speed Aggregate BW B200 nodes (31) 31 NDR 400G 1,550 GB/s RTX nodes (19) 38 NDR 400G 1,900 GB/s Service nodes (32) 32 NDR 400G 1,600 GB/s VAST CBoxes (14) 28 NDR 200G 700 GB/s UFM appliances (2) 4 NDR 400G 200 GB/s

RTX nodes have 2 IB ports on this fabric, used flexibly for both storage and MPI. The 100 GB/s aggregate per RTX node is ample for VAST parallel I/O and moderate-scale distributed training. Service nodes each have 1 IB port, ensuring that Globus transfers and file staging run at full InfiniBand speed to VAST.

docs.title: 3. Core Ethernet
docs.text:
Handles management, provisioning, user SSH sessions, web portals (OnDemand), and IP-based services. Does not carry GPU compute or storage I/O — those run over InfiniBand.

Property Value Switches 2x NVIDIA SN4700 (Spectrum-3, MLAG pair) GPU nodes 2x 25 GbE each (lightweight management) Service nodes 2x 100 GbE each (gateway role, 4x faster) VAST Ethernet CBoxes 400 GbE (IP-based NFS/S3 access)

Service nodes get 100 GbE because they are the front door — user SSH, OnDemand web traffic, and Globus transfers all flow through these Ethernet connections. GPU nodes only need 25 GbE for management since their heavy I/O runs over InfiniBand.

docs.title: 4. Out-of-Band Management
docs.text:
Dedicated hardware management access independent of the operating system, connecting to each node's BMC (iDRAC on Dell servers).

Property Value Switches 9x NVIDIA SN2201 (1 per rack) Ports per switch 48x 1 GbE + 4x 100 GbE uplink Total endpoints 107 (all nodes + VAST devices) Purpose iDRAC, remote console, power control, firmware updates

The OOB network is intentionally separate from all data and management traffic. Even if InfiniBand and Ethernet are completely down, administrators can reach every device via its BMC.

docs.title: Fabric Management: NVIDIA UFM
docs.text:
All InfiniBand switches are managed by 4 NVIDIA R660 UFM appliances in redundant pairs:

UFM Pair Managed Fabric Switches 2x R660 B200 rail-optimized IB 12 switches 2x R660 Cluster IB 6 switches

UFM provides real-time fabric monitoring, topology discovery, adaptive routing, SHARP (in-network allreduce acceleration), cable diagnostics, and telemetry streaming.

docs.title: Aggregate Bandwidth
docs.text: Fabric Total Endpoint BW Primary Traffic B200 Rail IB 12,400 GB/s GPU training (allreduce) Cluster IB 5,950 GB/s Storage I/O, MPI Core Ethernet ~230 Gb/s Management, user access OOB ~107 Mb/s Hardware control

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• B200 Nodes — Tier-1 compute with dedicated GPU fabric
  • RTX PRO 6000 Nodes — Tier-2 compute on shared fabric
  • Storage — VAST Data platform
  • Multi-GPU Computing — NCCL and InfiniBand for training

docs.title: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 GPU Nodes
docs.text: The RTX nodes provide broad-access GPU computing — 19 Dell PowerEdge XE7745 air-cooled servers, each containing 8 NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs in PCIe slots. Together they provide 152 RTX PRO 6000 GPUs with 14.25 TB of GPU memory and 17.8 PFLOP/s of FP32 compute.

docs.title: Per-Node Specifications
docs.text: Component Specification Server Dell PowerEdge XE7745 (4U, air-cooled + rear-door heat exchangers) CPUs 2x AMD EPYC 9555 (Turin, Zen 5) — 128 cores / 256 threads, 3.20 GHz GPUs 8x NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 BSE (PCIe Gen5 x16, Blackwell GB202) — CC 12.0 GPU Memory 96 GB GDDR7 per GPU — 768 GB per node GPU Memory Bandwidth 1,597 GB/s per GPU — 12.8 TB/s per node System RAM 24x 32 GB DDR5-6400 = 768 GB Local NVMe 1x 1.6 TB (mixed-use) FP32 per GPU 117 TFLOPS Tensor Cores 5th generation (FP4/FP8/FP6 micro-tensor scaling) Inter-GPU PCIe Gen5 x16 (~64 GB/s per GPU) — no NVLink Power ~7,900 W max per node Cooling Air-cooled with rear-door heat exchangers (RDHX)

docs.title: Network Interfaces
docs.text:
Interface Speed Purpose 2x ConnectX-7 NDR 400 Gb/s each Cluster IB fabric (VAST + MPI) 2x 25 GbE 25 Gb/s each In-band management / provisioning 1x 1 GbE (iDRAC) 1 Gb/s Out-of-band management

RTX nodes have 2 InfiniBand connections to the shared cluster IB fabric — used for both storage access and inter-node MPI. Unlike the B200 nodes, there is no dedicated GPU data fabric.

docs.title: How RTX Differs from B200
docs.text:
Property RTX PRO 6000 B200 GPU Memory 96 GB GDDR7 180 GB HBM3e Memory Bandwidth 1,597 GB/s 8,000 GB/s FP32 per GPU 117 TFLOPS 75 TFLOPS Inter-GPU (intra-node) PCIe Gen5 (64 GB/s) NVLink 5 (1,800 GB/s) Inter-node fabric Shared cluster IB (100 GB/s) Dedicated 8-rail IB (400 GB/s) Compute Capability 12.0 10.0 Cooling Air + RDHX Direct liquid Power per node ~7,900 W 12,200 W

The RTX PRO 6000 actually has higher FP32 throughput than the B200. The B200's advantages are in memory bandwidth (5x), memory capacity (1.9x), and NVLink interconnect (28x faster than PCIe). The RTX excels at compute-bound workloads that fit within 96 GB per GPU.

Different Compute Capabilities

Despite both being Blackwell-generation, the B200 (CC 10.0, data-center family) and RTX PRO 6000 (CC 12.0, professional family) have distinct SM designs. Code compiled with nvcc should target the correct architecture for each tier.

docs.title: GDDR7 vs HBM3e
docs.text:
Property GDDR7 (RTX) HBM3e (B200) Capacity per GPU 96 GB 180 GB Bandwidth per GPU 1,597 GB/s 8,000 GB/s Bus width Narrow, high-clocked (PAM4) 8192-bit, 3D-stacked Cost Moderate Very high

GDDR7 uses PAM4 signaling to achieve ~1,600 GB/s — a 1.85x improvement over previous-generation GDDR6. For compute-bound workloads, GDDR7 is more than sufficient. For bandwidth-intensive workloads (large-batch inference, attention-heavy models), HBM3e has a clear advantage.

docs.title: Aggregate (19 Nodes, 152 GPUs)
docs.text: Resource Total GPU Memory (GDDR7) 14.25 TB GPU Memory Bandwidth 242.7 TB/s System RAM (DDR5) ~14.25 TB FP32 Performance 17.8 PFLOP/s CPU Cores 2,432 IB Bandwidth (storage/MPI) 1.9 TB/s Power (servers only) ~150 kW

docs.title: Typical Workloads
docs.text: Use Case Why RTX Fits Inference serving 96 GB per GPU holds large models; 117 TFLOPS FP32 Single-node training 8 GPUs x 96 GB = 768 GB GPU memory per node Multi-node training (moderate scale) 2x NDR 400Gb IB per node for MPI Interactive development Jupyter, VS Code remote, debugging Prototyping before B200 Same Blackwell architecture — code runs on both

docs.title: Slurm Partitions
docs.text: Partition Nodes Max Time Default Time Use Case rtx-batch a0001–a0017 24h 1h Production jobs rtx-devel a0018–a0019 4h 15 min Development and testing

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• B200 Nodes — the Tier-1 GPU nodes
  • Networking — fabric topology
  • GPU Overview — choosing between B200 and RTX
  • GPU Jobs — requesting GPUs in Slurm

docs.title: Service Nodes
docs.text: The service block is the operational backbone of AICR — 32 Dell PowerEdge R6715 single-socket 1U servers providing 4,096 CPU cores and 36 TB of RAM with no GPUs. These nodes run everything that keeps the GPU tiers productive: login sessions, job scheduling, data transfer, monitoring, and the cpu Slurm partition for analysis workloads.

docs.title: Per-Node Specifications
docs.text: Component Specification Server Dell PowerEdge R6715 (1U, air-cooled, single-socket) CPU 1x AMD EPYC 9745 (Turin, Zen 5) — 128 cores / 256 threads, 2.40 GHz System RAM 12x 96 GB DDR5-6400 = 1,152 GB (1.125 TB) Local NVMe 1x 800 GB (mixed-use) L3 Cache Up to 512 MB Power 813 W max Cooling Air-cooled (1U internal fans + rear-door heat exchangers)

docs.title: Why Single-Socket?
docs.text: Each EPYC 9745 has 128 cores in one socket — matching the total core count of the dual-socket GPU nodes. The single-socket design eliminates NUMA penalties (all memory is local to the single socket), simplifies memory topology, and keeps power under 815 W per node. The entire 32-node service block draws roughly the same power as two B200 GPU nodes.

docs.title: Network Interfaces
docs.text:
Interface Speed Purpose 1x ConnectX-7 NDR 400 Gb/s Cluster IB fabric (VAST storage) 2x 100 GbE 100 Gb/s each Management, user SSH, web portals 1x 1 GbE (iDRAC) 1 Gb/s Out-of-band management

Service nodes have 100 GbE Ethernet (4x faster than the GPU nodes' 25 GbE), reflecting their role as the gateway between users and the cluster. The InfiniBand connection ensures that data transfers via Globus and file staging run at full storage speed.

docs.title: Node Roles
docs.text:
The 32 nodes are assigned to four functional groups:

Role Nodes Description General service 22 Slurm controllers, login gateways, OnDemand, Globus, monitoring, provisioning, and CPU compute via the cpu Slurm partition Proxmox 4 Dev/test virtualization platform for the systems team Public-facing 3 External-facing services: OnDemand portal, Globus endpoints, SSH gateways Collaboration 3 Shared environments for multi-institutional research teams

The general service nodes serve double duty: some run cluster infrastructure services (Slurm, monitoring, provisioning), while the rest are available as Slurm-schedulable CPU resources for data analysis, preprocessing, and workflow orchestration.

docs.title: Aggregate (32 Nodes)
docs.text: Resource Total CPU Cores 4,096 CPU Threads 8,192 System RAM (DDR5) 36 TB IB Bandwidth to VAST 1.6 TB/s Ethernet Bandwidth 6.4 Tb/s (2x 100 GbE per node) Power ~26 kW

docs.title: Slurm Partition
docs.text:
Partition Max Time Default Time Use Case cpu 24h 15 min Data analysis, Dask, workflow orchestration

The cpu partition provides high-memory, high-core-count nodes for work that doesn't need GPUs. See Analysis and Data Processing.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• B200 Nodes — the Tier-1 GPU nodes
  • RTX PRO 6000 Nodes — the Tier-2 GPU nodes
  • Analysis and Data Processing — using the CPU partition
  • Networking — how service nodes connect to the cluster

docs.title: Storage: VAST Data Platform
docs.text:
All persistent storage on AICR is served by a single VAST Data system delivering approximately 14.3 PiB of effective capacity with a peak throughput of 360 GB/s. The system occupies one rack and is accessed by every node in the cluster over InfiniBand.

VAST replaces the traditional approach of separate scratch and archive tiers with a single platform that serves all roles from a unified namespace.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
VAST uses a Disaggregated Shared Everything (DASE) architecture that separates compute and storage into independently scalable layers:

  • CBoxes (16) — stateless protocol servers that handle client I/O, metadata, and data reduction (compression). Any CBox can serve any request for any file; if one fails, clients reconnect to another transparently.
  • DBoxes (7) — dense NVMe flash enclosures (all-flash, no spinning disks) holding all persistent data, protected by global erasure coding.
  • NVMe-oF fabric (2 switches) — internal mesh connecting CBoxes to DBoxes at full NVMe speed.

docs.title: Hardware Summary
docs.text: Component Quantity Role CBox (InfiniBand) 14 High-performance protocol servers, IB-connected CBox (Ethernet) 2 IP-based protocol servers (NFS/TCP, S3) DBox 7 NVMe flash enclosures NVMe-oF Switch 2 Internal CBox-to-DBox fabric

docs.title: Capacity
docs.text:
Metric Value Raw physical capacity ~8.5 PiB Usable capacity (after erasure coding) ~8.2 PiB Effective user capacity (with 1.8:1 compression) ~14.3 PiB Data reduction ratio 1.8:1 (inline compression) Peak throughput 360 GB/s

The effective capacity depends on data compressibility — structured data and text compress well (higher ratio), while pre-compressed formats (JPEG, video) approach 1:1.

docs.title: How Clients Access Storage
docs.text:
Every node in the cluster connects to VAST through the shared InfiniBand cluster fabric:

Node Type IB Ports to VAST Aggregate Link BW B200 (31 nodes) 1x NDR 400G each 1,550 GB/s RTX (19 nodes) 2x NDR 400G each 1,900 GB/s Service (32 nodes) 1x NDR 400G each 1,600 GB/s

Client-side bandwidth (5+ TB/s) far exceeds VAST's peak throughput (360 GB/s). This is intentional — the fabric is never the bottleneck, and individual nodes see low latency even under load.

docs.title: Protocols
docs.text:
VAST supports multiple access protocols simultaneously on the same data:

Protocol Interface Typical Use NFS (RDMA) InfiniBand CBoxes Primary POSIX filesystem for compute nodes NFS (TCP) Ethernet CBoxes External data access, IP-based clients S3 Ethernet CBoxes Object storage for ML frameworks, data pipelines GPU Direct Storage InfiniBand CBoxes Direct DMA from storage to GPU memory, bypassing CPU

docs.title: GPU Direct Storage
docs.text: VAST supports NVIDIA GPU Direct Storage (GDS), which moves data directly from the storage system into GPU memory over InfiniBand — bypassing the CPU entirely. This reduces latency and frees CPU resources for other work. Both B200 and RTX nodes support GDS.

docs.title: Data Protection
docs.text:
Feature Configuration Erasure coding DATA_10_PARITY_2 (10 data + 2 parity strips per stripe) Failure tolerance Any 2 simultaneous drive/component failures Encryption at rest Enabled (all data on NVMe drives) Compression Inline, always-on Deduplication Available but currently disabled Snapshots Space-efficient, instantaneous, writeable

The 10+2 erasure coding has ~17% parity overhead — significantly more space-efficient than mirroring (50%) or triple replication (67%).

docs.title: Storage Tiers (as seen by users)
docs.text:
VAST presents a single storage pool, but AICR allocates capacity across logical tiers:

Tier Path Quota Snapshots Purpose Home /home/USERNAME 100 GiB 7-day Code, configs, small environments Scratch /scratch/USERNAME ~10 TiB (~15M file limit) None Active job I/O, checkpoints Shared /apps/aicr, /apps/<institution> Centrally managed 7-day Software modules, shared datasets Work /work/INSTITUTION/PROJECTNAME Per-project 7-day Project data, collaboration

All tiers live on the same hardware and share the same namespace. The distinction is administrative (quotas, policies) rather than physical. Data doesn't need to be migrated between tiers.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Filesystem Overview — quotas, snapshots, and purge policies
  • Transferring Data — Globus, scp, rsync
  • Large Datasets — data staging and I/O optimization
  • Networking — how storage connects to the cluster
